stories.headline: Iran-U.S. War Escalates as Tehran Fires Missiles at Gulf States, Israel; Trump Issues Fresh 48-Hour Ultimatum
stories.summary:
The conflict between the United States and Iran intensified Saturday as Iran launched additional missile barrages targeting Gulf states including Kuwait, Bahrain, and the UAE, as well as Israel. Kuwait reported that Iranian drone strikes knocked out two power generation stations and damaged water desalination plants, with earlier attacks also striking a government office complex and sparking a fire at the Kuwaiti oil ministry. The U.S. and Israel continued their bombardment of Iranian military, energy, and industrial sites.

President Donald Trump renewed his threat on Truth Social, warning that 'all Hell will reign down on' Iran if it did not reopen the Strait of Hormuz or agree to a deal within 48 hours. Iranian officials rejected the ultimatum outright, with Gen. Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi of Iran's central military command calling Trump's statement 'helpless, nervous, unbalanced and stupid' and warning that 'the gates of hell will open for you' if hostilities escalated further.

The standoff continued against the backdrop of a search for a missing American weapons systems officer after a U.S. F-15E was shot down over Iran on Friday. U.S. media reported the pilot was rescued, while Iranian state media said at least four people were killed in a U.S.-Israeli airstrike in the same region. Unverified social media videos showed Iranians converging on a mountainous area of southwestern Iran to search for the missing American crew member, with Iranian officials offering bounties for capture.

The broadening conflict has drawn in multiple regional actors and raised alarm among humanitarian organizations, who warn that disruptions to the Strait of Hormuz and key logistics hubs in Dubai and Doha are severely hampering global aid delivery. The United Nations has described the supply chain disruption as the most significant since COVID-19, with shipping costs rising as much as 20 percent and deliveries rerouted around Africa, adding weeks to transit times.

stories.headline: Iran War Disrupts Global Humanitarian Aid Supply Chains, Relief Groups Warn
stories.summary:
Major international aid organizations are warning that the U.S.-Iran war and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz have severely disrupted global humanitarian supply chains, threatening food and medicine delivery to millions of vulnerable people worldwide. The World Food Program says tens of thousands of metric tons of food are heavily delayed in transit, while the International Rescue Committee reports $130,000 worth of pharmaceuticals for Sudan stranded in Dubai and nearly 670 boxes of therapeutic food for malnourished children in Somalia stuck in India.

Key shipping routes through the Strait of Hormuz and logistics hubs in Dubai, Doha, and Abu Dhabi have been significantly impacted, forcing aid groups to reroute vessels around Africa or use expensive hybrid land-sea-air transport options. The United Nations has characterized the disruption as the most significant to global supply chains since COVID-19, with costs rising up to 20 percent and delivery timelines stretching by weeks or more.

UNICEF reported it is now flying vaccines to Turkey and driving them into Iran rather than shipping directly, sharply increasing costs for vaccination campaigns. Aid officials cautioned that even after fighting stops, the shock to supply chains could continue delaying lifesaving assistance for months.

The crisis compounds existing strains on international aid budgets, which had already been severely weakened by steep U.S. cuts to foreign assistance. Relief organizations are urging an immediate ceasefire and restoration of shipping routes, warning that the confluence of funding cuts and supply chain collapse risks pushing humanitarian operations 'beyond their limits,' according to the International Rescue Committee.

stories.headline: DHS and Democratic Senator Clash Over ICE Arrest of Honduran Man in Baltimore
stories.summary:
The Department of Homeland Security and Sen. Chris Van Hollen, D-Md., offered sharply conflicting accounts Saturday of an Immigration and Customs Enforcement arrest incident in Baltimore that left a man hospitalized. DHS identified the individual as Ever Omar Alvarenga-Rios, a Honduran national with a final order of removal dating to 2018, and said he recklessly evaded officers, caused a multi-vehicle crash, and sent two ICE agents to the hospital. The agency said officers used the minimum force necessary to complete the arrest.

Van Hollen posted photographs of the man in a hospital bed on social media, describing him as an 'asylum seeker' who was rear-ended by an ICE vehicle while driving to work and who suffered significant injuries to his head, chest, back, and hands. The senator also alleged ICE was denying the man access to attorneys, which DHS did not directly address in its public statement.

DHS Acting Assistant Secretary Lauren Bis said in a statement that the man 'broke our laws, resisted arrest, sent two ICE law enforcement officers to the hospital, and endangered the general public,' adding that both officers are expected to make full recoveries. DHS also used the incident to criticize what it called 'sanctuary politicians' who it said encourage illegal immigrants to evade arrest through informational webinars.

The dispute reflects a broader national debate over the conduct and scope of immigration enforcement operations. Immigrant rights advocates argue that aggressive enforcement tactics risk harming individuals with legitimate legal claims, while administration supporters contend that enforcement of standing removal orders is both lawful and necessary for public safety.

stories.headline: Iran War Escalates: Drone and Missile Strikes Hit Kuwait, Bahrain, and Lebanon as A-10 Aircraft Reported Hit
stories.summary:
The U.S.-Iran conflict continued to expand Saturday, with drone attacks causing significant damage to Kuwait's Finance Ministry complex and striking energy storage facilities in Bahrain. Kuwait's Electricity Ministry reported that two power generation units were taken out of service following Sunday's attack, while Bapco Energies said a fire at its storage facilities was extinguished with no injuries reported. Iran's state media separately claimed an A-10 aircraft was hit by Iranian fire, adding to earlier reports of U.S. aircraft losses in the conflict.

Israeli airstrikes continued in Lebanon, striking residential areas in the southern port city of Tyre and the village of Saksakiyeh. Images from the region showed widespread destruction, with displaced Lebanese families sheltering in Beirut. Protests calling for an end to the war drew crowds in Tel Aviv, where residents also sought shelter in bomb shelters during Iranian missile strikes.

The conflict has begun to reverberate beyond the immediate theater. Bangladesh imposed energy austerity measures, cutting office hours and mall opening hours, as it seeks alternative fuel sources and external financing in response to disruptions caused by the war. The attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure signal an expanding front that threatens global oil and gas supplies.

Iranian judiciary media reported the arrest of two individuals, identified as Mohammad Ami Biglari and Shahin Vahedparast, as Iran continues to manage internal security amid the ongoing conflict. The war, now in its fifth week, has drawn in multiple regional actors and continues to escalate with no ceasefire in sight.

stories.headline: Trump's 2027 Budget Proposes 44% Defense Spending Increase, Deep Cuts to Domestic Programs
stories.summary:
President Donald Trump released his 2027 budget proposal Friday, requesting $1.5 trillion for the Pentagon — a 44% increase over current levels and the largest such request in decades. The plan, which Budget Director Russell Vought described as fulfilling Trump's promise to reinvest in national security, also calls for a 10% reduction in non-defense discretionary spending. The budget reflects the administration's wartime posture as the U.S.-led conflict with Iran enters its fifth week.

Among the proposed cuts are a 19% reduction in the Agriculture Department budget, a 13% cut for Housing and Urban Development, and roughly a 12% decrease for Health and Human Services, including elimination of a low-income home heating assistance program. The plan also cancels more than $15 billion in Biden-era infrastructure law spending, including renewable energy and NOAA grants, while ending certain university grant programs.

On the spending side, the proposal includes a $10 billion fund for construction and beautification projects in Washington, D.C., a $481 million increase in aviation safety and air traffic control hiring, and sustained funding for immigration enforcement operations including 100,000 adult detention beds and 30,000 family beds. Trump told a private White House event that wartime conditions make domestic social programs a lower federal priority, suggesting states should assume responsibility for programs like Medicaid and day care.

The president's annual budget is a statement of priorities and does not carry the force of law; Congress is free to reject it and frequently does. The proposal is expected to face significant resistance even within Republican ranks, particularly from members representing rural districts that rely on Agriculture Department funding and low-income energy assistance. Democrats have broadly criticized the proposed domestic cuts as harmful to working families.

stories.headline: Detroit Pistons Clinch No. 1 Eastern Conference Seed for First Time Since 2007
stories.summary:
The Detroit Pistons clinched the top seed in the Eastern Conference playoffs Saturday night, beating the Philadelphia 76ers 116-93. It marks the first time the franchise has held the East's top seed since the 2006-07 season, representing a dramatic turnaround for a team that endured years of rebuilding. The Pistons improved to 56-21 on the season with the victory.

Tobias Harris led Detroit with 19 points, while Daniss Jenkins contributed 16 points and 14 assists. Jalen Duren added 16 points and Ausar Thompson chipped in 14, with the Pistons building a commanding second-half lead after outscoring Philadelphia in the final minutes of the first half. The win is particularly notable as it came without star guard Cade Cunningham, who has missed 10 games with a collapsed left lung; Detroit is 8-2 in those contests.

For Philadelphia, Tyrese Maxey led all scorers with 23 points, and Paul George added 20 in his continued return from a 25-game drug policy suspension. The 76ers played without center Joel Embiid, who was held out with a right oblique injury. Philadelphia had won eight of its previous 11 games entering the matchup.

The Pistons' rise to the conference's top seed is one of the notable stories of the NBA season, coming after years of lottery finishes. Detroit will host home-court advantage throughout the Eastern Conference playoffs.

stories.headline: Federal Judge Halts White House Ballroom Project, Ruling Construction Requires Congressional Approval
stories.summary:
A federal judge this week ordered a halt to construction of President Trump's planned 90,000-square-foot White House ballroom, ruling that the president lacks the statutory authority to proceed without Congressional approval. Judge Leon, in a 35-page ruling, wrote that the president is "the steward of the White House for future generations of First Families" but "not the owner," adding that no statute grants the authority claimed by the administration. The Department of Justice has appealed the decision.

Despite the ruling, the National Capital Planning Commission proceeded with a vote Thursday to approve the project, with commission chair Will Scharf stating the judge's order only prevented construction activity rather than planning deliberations. The commission voted 8-1 in favor, with Republican Sen. Rand Paul of Kentucky casting a yes vote — drawing rare praise from Trump, who called Paul "an extraordinarily difficult vote" and publicly thanked him on Truth Social.

The ballroom, which is being funded by private donors rather than taxpayer funds, is part of what the White House calls an East Wing modernization project. Construction began after the East Wing was demolished in October 2025. The project is slated for completion by 2028, though the timeline is uncertain pending the outcome of the legal challenge.

The clash between the executive and judicial branches over the project reflects broader tensions during the Trump administration over the limits of presidential authority over federal property. Critics have argued the project bypasses required legislative oversight, while the administration maintains that private funding and presidential stewardship of the White House justify the construction.
